title: Why Start with Logic Gates?
concept: To understand where neural networks came from, it helps to zoom all the way down to the simplest possible decision-building blocks: logic gates, the tiny true/false rules that inspired early machine intelligence and led toward the perceptron.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why not choose deep learning for every problem?

options.text: Because it can require more data, compute, training time, and tuning
Because it cannot learn patterns from raw data
Because it only works for history class

exercises.explain: Right. Deep learning is powerful, but to really understand it, we now rewind to the simplest decision rules machines ever used.

cards.title: From giant networks back to tiny decisions
cards.body: We just talked about modern neural networks with many layers and millions of weights. So why suddenly rewind? Because if you zoom in far enough, all machine decision-making starts with a much smaller question: how can a machine turn simple inputs into a yes/no output?

cards.title: Why logic gates come first
cards.body: A logic gate is a tiny true/false rule, and early computers were built from huge collections of them. Studying gates first shows how complex decisions can be built from simple yes/no pieces — which makes the later leap to a learned decision unit, like a perceptron, much easier to understand.

cards.title: Bricks before skyscrapers
cards.body: If you want to understand a skyscraper, it helps to first understand bricks, beams, and how simple pieces connect. Logic gates are like the bricks of machine decision-making: tiny on their own, but powerful when combined.

cards.title: A rule wired by hand
cards.body: Imagine an alarm that should ring only if the door is open AND it is nighttime. That is a tiny hand-built decision rule. Early machines used many rules like this, wired in advance. Researchers then asked a bigger question: could a machine learn gate-like decisions from examples instead of having every rule manually built?

cards.title: This is not a random hardware detour
cards.body: Studying logic gates is not just old-computer trivia. It is the cleanest historical bridge from fixed, hand-written rules to the first learned neural decision units. In other words: it explains what problem the perceptron was trying to solve.

cards.title: The bridge into Unit 58
cards.body: One question connects this unit to the next one:

left.text:
Humans define each true/false rule
by hand, one at a time

right.text:
Could a machine learn similar rules
from examples instead?

exercises.prompt: Why is a unit on logic gates placed before perceptrons?

options.text: Because gates are the simplest building blocks of machine decisions
Because logic gates are a completely unrelated topic
Because perceptrons were invented before simple true/false rules
Because deep learning never uses simple decisions

exercises.explain: Exactly. Starting small makes the later neural story easier to follow.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Studying logic gates helps explain the question neural networks were trying to answer — can machines learn rules instead of having every rule wired by hand?
exercises.explain: True. That question is the bridge from hard-coded logic to learned models.

exercises.sentence: We start with logic gates because they are tiny decision ___ that make later neural ideas easier to understand.
exercises.explain: Yes. Small building blocks make the bigger system less mysterious.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best bridges into the next unit?

options.text: Early machines used fixed true/false gates, and researchers wondered whether a machine could learn similar decisions from examples
Logic gates made training data unnecessary forever
Deep learning only works when humans wire every rule by hand

exercises.explain: Right. That historical question is exactly why logic gates are the perfect next stop.

exercises.prompt: If deep neural networks are so modern and powerful, why would a course rewind to logic gates before introducing perceptrons?

options.text: Because understanding the simplest hand-built true/false decisions makes the leap to learned decision units much easier and more historical
Because deep learning and logic have nothing to do with each other
Because logic gates are more advanced than neural networks
Because perceptrons came before simple true/false rules

exercises.explain: Exactly. Before a machine could learn a rule, humans first had to understand how to build one. Next up in Unit 58: the simplest rule-builders of all — AND, OR, and NOT logic gates.
